Name: geometric_mixed_precision_quantization
Title: Geometric Insights for Training-Free Mixed Precision Quantization
Short Hypothesis: Can we utilize geometric properties of weight distributions in neural networks to enhance the effectiveness of training-free mixed precision quantization? This approach aims to reveal hidden correlations between layer characteristics and optimal quantization schemes, significantly improving quantization accuracy without the need for extensive retraining.
Related Work: Existing frameworks like EMQ and InfoQ primarily rely on evolutionary strategies or mutual information metrics, but they do not exploit the underlying geometry of layer weight distributions. This proposal distinguishes itself by focusing on geometric insights from linear algebra and curvature-aware methods, providing a novel perspective that has not been previously explored in the context of mixed precision quantization.
Abstract: Mixed Precision Quantization (MPQ) has emerged as a vital technique for deploying deep neural networks efficiently; however, training-based methods often suffer from high computational costs and inefficiencies. In this proposal, we introduce a novel framework that leverages geometric properties of weight distributions in neural networks to inform the quantization process. We hypothesize that by analyzing the curvature and distributional characteristics of weights across layers, we can derive more effective quantization schemes that minimize performance degradation. Our approach will involve the development of a curvature-aware optimization proxy that assesses the sensitivity of weights to quantization based on their geometric attributes. We will validate this framework through experiments on popular architectures, measuring quantization accuracy and computational efficiency against existing state-of-the-art methods. We anticipate that this approach will not only improve quantization accuracy but also provide deeper insights into the interplay between weight geometry and quantization strategies.
Experiments: 1. Conduct a sensitivity analysis on various neural network architectures to quantify the geometric properties of weights. 2. Implement a curvature-aware optimization routine for quantization, applying it to a range of models (e.g., ResNet, MobileNet). 3. Compare the performance of our geometric quantization framework against existing training-free methods like EMQ and InfoQ in terms of accuracy drop and computational overhead. 4. Evaluate the impact of geometric insights on layer-wise quantization, assessing how layer characteristics influence overall model performance. Metrics: Accuracy drop, FLOPs reduction, and inference time.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The primary risk lies in the potential complexity of accurately capturing the geometric properties of weights in high-dimensional spaces. Additionally, while geometric insights offer a promising direction, there is a possibility that they may not yield substantial improvements over existing methods. Limitations may include the need for domain-specific adjustments when applying the geometric framework to various architectures.
